items.correct: Gradually increasing training demands so the body keeps adapting.
items.misconception: Training as hard as possible immediately.
items.why: Students think 'overload' means 'maximum'. It means slightly exceeding current capacity - too much causes injury; too little stalls progress. The key word is PROGRESSIVE.

items.q: What is the difference between aerobic and muscular endurance?
items.correct: Aerobic endurance is the cardiovascular system's ability to supply oxygen; muscular endurance is a muscle's ability to contract repeatedly without fatigue.
items.misconception: They are different names for the same component.
items.random_wrong: Aerobic is for legs, muscular for arms.
items.why: Students treat both as 'being able to keep going'. Aerobic endurance is WHOLE-BODY cardio capacity; muscular endurance is LOCALISED to specific muscles (e.g., sit-up reps).

items.q: What does the 'S' in SPORT stand for?
items.correct: Specificity - training should match the demands of the sport.
items.why: Students guess fitness components. SPORT = Specificity, Progression, Overload, Reversibility, Tedium (or Time). Specificity means training matches what the athlete needs (sprinter doesn't train like a marathoner).

items.q: Which method is BEST for time-efficient training?
items.why: It's HIIT. “Continuous” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What does cross training help reduce?
items.why: Correct answer: Risk of overuse injury. “VO2 max” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A typical HIIT work interval is about how many seconds?
items.why: The real figure is 30. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
